##=============================================================================
## POSTGRESQL DATABASE MODULE
##=============================================================================
## COMMENTED OUT - Cost Optimization (Re-enable in 2-3 weeks as needed)
## PostgreSQL Flexible Server for development environment will be re-enabled when needed
## To enable: Uncomment resources below and PostgreSQL env vars in Container App
##=============================================================================
## POSTGRESQL DATABASE MODULE
##=============================================================================
## PostgreSQL Flexible Server for development environment
## Re-enabled for Keycloak deployment
## Databases: intellxxdna_dev (main app) + keycloak (Keycloak identity service)
##

# PostgreSQL Flexible Server
resource "azurerm_postgresql_flexible_server" "postgres" {

  name                = "${local.resource_prefix}-psql"
  resource_group_name = azurerm_resource_group.rg.name
  location            = var.azure_region

  # Deployment
  version                = "14"
  administrator_login    = var.postgres_admin_username
  administrator_password = var.postgres_admin_password

  # Compute & Storage
  sku_name   = "B_Standard_B2s"
  storage_mb = 65536 # 64 GiB in MB

  # Backup
  backup_retention_days        = 7
  geo_redundant_backup_enabled = false

  # Network
  delegated_subnet_id           = module.networking.database_subnet_id
  private_dns_zone_id           = azurerm_private_dns_zone.postgres.id
  public_network_access_enabled = false

  auto_grow_enabled = false

  tags = local.tags

  lifecycle {
    ignore_changes = [zone]
  }

  depends_on = [azurerm_private_dns_zone.postgres,
  azurerm_private_dns_zone_virtual_network_link.postgres]
}

# Private DNS Zone for PostgreSQL
resource "azurerm_private_dns_zone" "postgres" {

  name                = "dev.postgres.database.azure.com"
  resource_group_name = azurerm_resource_group.rg.name

  tags = local.tags
}

# Link Private DNS Zone to VNet
resource "azurerm_private_dns_zone_virtual_network_link" "postgres" {

  name                  = "${local.resource_prefix}-postgres-vnet-link"
  resource_group_name   = azurerm_resource_group.rg.name
  private_dns_zone_name = azurerm_private_dns_zone.postgres.name
  virtual_network_id    = module.networking.vnet_id

  depends_on = [azurerm_private_dns_zone.postgres]
}

# Database for the application
resource "azurerm_postgresql_flexible_server_database" "app_database" {

  name      = "intellxxdna"
  server_id = azurerm_postgresql_flexible_server.postgres.id
  charset   = "UTF8"
  collation = "en_US.utf8"

  depends_on = [azurerm_postgresql_flexible_server.postgres]
}

# Database for Keycloak
resource "azurerm_postgresql_flexible_server_database" "keycloak_database" {

  name      = "keycloak"
  server_id = azurerm_postgresql_flexible_server.postgres.id
  charset   = "UTF8"
  collation = "en_US.utf8"

  depends_on = [azurerm_postgresql_flexible_server.postgres]
}


##=============================================================================
## KEY VAULT SECRETS - DATABASE CREDENTIALS
##=============================================================================
## PostgreSQL connection parameters stored securely in Key Vault
## Re-enabled for Keycloak and application database access
##=============================================================================

# PostgreSQL Host
resource "azurerm_key_vault_secret" "postgres_host" {
  name         = "postgres-host"
  value        = azurerm_postgresql_flexible_server.postgres.fqdn
  key_vault_id = module.storage.key_vault_id

  depends_on = [azurerm_postgresql_flexible_server.postgres]
}

# PostgreSQL Port
resource "azurerm_key_vault_secret" "postgres_port" {
  name         = "postgres-port"
  value        = "5432"
  key_vault_id = module.storage.key_vault_id
}

# PostgreSQL Database Name
resource "azurerm_key_vault_secret" "lexxi_postgres_db" {
  name         = "lexxi-postgres-db"
  value        = "intellxxdna"
  key_vault_id = module.storage.key_vault_id
}

# PostgreSQL Admin Username
resource "azurerm_key_vault_secret" "postgres_user" {
  name         = "postgres-user"
  value        = var.postgres_admin_username
  key_vault_id = module.storage.key_vault_id
}

# PostgreSQL Admin Password
resource "azurerm_key_vault_secret" "postgres_password" {
  name         = "postgres-password"
  value        = var.postgres_admin_password
  key_vault_id = module.storage.key_vault_id
}

# RD PostgreSQL URL (Full connection string)
resource "azurerm_key_vault_secret" "intellxxdna_postgres_url" {
  name         = "rd-postgres-url"
  value        = "postgresql+asyncpg://${var.intellxxdna_admin_username}:${urlencode(var.intellxxdna_admin_password)}@${azurerm_postgresql_flexible_server.postgres.fqdn}:5432/intellxxdna"
  key_vault_id = module.storage.key_vault_id

  depends_on = [azurerm_postgresql_flexible_server.postgres]
}

# Disable SSL requirement
resource "azurerm_postgresql_flexible_server_configuration" "require_secure_transport" {
  name      = "require_secure_transport"
  server_id = azurerm_postgresql_flexible_server.postgres.id
  value     = "off"

  depends_on = [azurerm_postgresql_flexible_server.postgres]
}

# PostgreSQL Firewall Rule - Allow container-apps subnet
resource "azurerm_postgresql_flexible_server_firewall_rule" "container_apps" {

  name             = "AllowContainerAppsSubnet"
  server_id        = azurerm_postgresql_flexible_server.postgres.id
  start_ip_address = "10.0.3.0"
  end_ip_address   = "10.0.3.255"

  depends_on = [azurerm_postgresql_flexible_server.postgres]
}

# PostgreSQL Firewall Rule - Allow legacy-apps subnet
resource "azurerm_postgresql_flexible_server_firewall_rule" "legacy_apps" {

  name             = "AllowLegacyAppsSubnet"
  server_id        = azurerm_postgresql_flexible_server.postgres.id
  start_ip_address = "10.0.4.0"
  end_ip_address   = "10.0.4.255"

  depends_on = [azurerm_postgresql_flexible_server.postgres]
}



##=============================================================================
## CONTAINER APPS ENVIRONMENT & APPLICATION
##=============================================================================
## Azure Container Apps for containerized workloads
## Configuration:
## - Plan: Consumption (pay-per-use, auto-scaling)
## - Environment: Connected to container-apps subnet (10.0.3.0/24)
## - Image: lexxi from shared ACR (ixdnashared.azurecr.io)
## - Resources: 1 vCPU, 2GB memory
## - Replicas: Minimum 1, Maximum 3
##
## The Container Apps Environment integrates with the VNet for
## secure, isolated container deployments within the private network

# Container Apps Environment connected to VNet
resource "azurerm_container_app_environment" "env" {

  name                           = "${local.resource_prefix}-cae"
  location                       = var.azure_region
  resource_group_name            = azurerm_resource_group.rg.name
  log_analytics_workspace_id     = module.storage.log_analytics_workspace_id
  infrastructure_subnet_id       = module.networking.container_apps_subnet_id
  internal_load_balancer_enabled = false

  tags = local.tags

  depends_on = [module.networking, module.storage]

  lifecycle {
    ignore_changes = [tags, infrastructure_resource_group_name]
  }
}



#
# Container App - RD
#

# RD Swagger user name.
resource "azurerm_key_vault_secret" "rd_swagger_username" {
  name  = "rd-swagger-username"
  value = var.rd_swagger_username

  key_vault_id = module.storage.key_vault_id

  depends_on = [module.storage]
}

# RD Swagger user password.
resource "azurerm_key_vault_secret" "rd_swagger_pw" {
  name  = "rd-swagger-pw"
  value = var.rd_swagger_pw

  key_vault_id = module.storage.key_vault_id

  depends_on = [module.storage]
}

# RD environment.
resource "azurerm_key_vault_secret" "rd_environment" {
  name  = "rd-environment"
  value = var.rd_environment

  key_vault_id = module.storage.key_vault_id

  depends_on = [module.storage]
}

# RD JWT_SECRET_KEY.
resource "azurerm_key_vault_secret" "rd_jwt_secret_key" {
  name  = "rd-jwt-secret-key"
  value = var.rd_jwt_secret_key

  key_vault_id = module.storage.key_vault_id

  depends_on = [module.storage]
}

# RD CORS_ALLOW_ORIGINS.
resource "azurerm_key_vault_secret" "rd_cors_allow_origins" {
  name  = "rd-cors-allow-origins"
  value = var.rd_cors_allow_origins

  key_vault_id = module.storage.key_vault_id

  depends_on = [module.storage]
}

# RD ACCESS_TOKEN_EXPIRE_MINUTES.
resource "azurerm_key_vault_secret" "rd_access_token_expire_minutes" {
  name  = "rd-access-token-expire-minutes"
  value = var.rd_access_token_expire_minutes

  key_vault_id = module.storage.key_vault_id

  depends_on = [module.storage]
}

# RD Sentry DSN.
resource "azurerm_key_vault_secret" "rd_sentry_dsn" {
  name  = "rd-sentry-dsn"
  value = var.rd_sentry_dsn

  key_vault_id = module.storage.key_vault_id

  depends_on = [module.storage]
}

# RD Container app.
resource "azurerm_container_app" "rd" {

  name                         = "${local.resource_prefix}-rd"
  container_app_environment_id = azurerm_container_app_environment.env.id
  resource_group_name          = azurerm_resource_group.rg.name
  revision_mode                = "Single"

  # Enable managed identity for Key Vault secret access
  identity {
    type = "SystemAssigned"
  }

  # Enable ingress for public access
  ingress {

    allow_insecure_connections = true
    external_enabled           = true
    target_port                = 3000
    transport                  = "http"

    dynamic "ip_security_restriction" {
      for_each = { for idx, ip in local.front_door_backend_ips : "fd${idx}" => ip }
      content {
        name             = ip_security_restriction.key
        action           = "Allow"
        ip_address_range = ip_security_restriction.value
        description      = "Azure Front Door backend IP"
      }
    }

    traffic_weight {
      latest_revision = true
      percentage      = 100
    }
  }

  template {

    container {

      name   = "rd"
      image  = "${var.shared_container_registry_login_server}/${var.container_rd_app_image_name}:dev-latest"
      cpu    = var.container_app_cpu
      memory = var.container_app_memory

      # Database credentials from Key Vault
      # Note: Container Apps doesn't support @Microsoft.KeyVault() syntax
      # We fetch actual values from Key Vault via Terraform at apply time
      env {
        name  = "POSTGRES_URL"
        value = azurerm_key_vault_secret.intellxxdna_postgres_url.value
      }

      env {
        name  = "SWAGGER_USERNAME"
        value = azurerm_key_vault_secret.rd_swagger_username.value
      }

      env {
        name  = "SWAGGER_PASSWORD"
        value = azurerm_key_vault_secret.rd_swagger_pw.value
      }

      env {
        name  = "ENVIRONMENT"
        value = azurerm_key_vault_secret.rd_environment.value
      }

      env {
        name  = "JWT_SECRET_KEY"
        value = azurerm_key_vault_secret.rd_jwt_secret_key.value
      }

      env {
        name  = "CORS_ALLOW_ORIGINS"
        value = azurerm_key_vault_secret.rd_cors_allow_origins.value
      }

      env {
        name  = "ACCESS_TOKEN_EXPIRE_MINUTES"
        value = azurerm_key_vault_secret.rd_access_token_expire_minutes.value
      }

      env {
        name  = "SENTRY_DSN"
        value = azurerm_key_vault_secret.rd_sentry_dsn.value
      }
    }

    min_replicas = var.container_app_min_replicas
    max_replicas = var.container_app_max_replicas
  }

  registry {
    server               = var.shared_container_registry_login_server
    username             = var.shared_container_registry_username
    password_secret_name = "acr-password"
  }

  secret {
    name  = "acr-password"
    value = var.shared_container_registry_password
  }

  tags = local.tags

  lifecycle {
    ignore_changes = [workload_profile_name]
  }

  depends_on = [
    azurerm_container_app_environment.env,
    azurerm_postgresql_flexible_server.postgres,
    azurerm_key_vault_secret.intellxxdna_postgres_url
    # module.redis
  ]
}



#
# Container App - Lexxi
#

# Lexxi OpenAI token
resource "azurerm_key_vault_secret" "lexxi_openai_token" {
  name  = "lexxi-openai-token"
  value = var.lexxi_openai_token

  key_vault_id = module.storage.key_vault_id

  depends_on = [module.storage]
}

# Lexxi CORS allow origins
resource "azurerm_key_vault_secret" "lexxi_cors_allow_origins" {
  name  = "lexxi-cors-allow-origins"
  value = var.lexxi_cors_allow_origins

  key_vault_id = module.storage.key_vault_id

  depends_on = [module.storage]
}

# Lexxi Sentry DSN.
resource "azurerm_key_vault_secret" "lexxi_sentry_dsn" {
  name  = "lexxi-sentry-dsn"
  value = var.lexxi_sentry_dsn

  key_vault_id = module.storage.key_vault_id

  depends_on = [module.storage]
}

# Lexxi Container app.
resource "azurerm_container_app" "lexxi" {

  name                         = "${local.resource_prefix}-lexxi"
  container_app_environment_id = azurerm_container_app_environment.env.id
  resource_group_name          = azurerm_resource_group.rg.name
  revision_mode                = "Single"

  # Enable managed identity for Key Vault secret access
  identity {
    type = "SystemAssigned"
  }

  # Enable ingress for public access
  ingress {

    allow_insecure_connections = true
    external_enabled           = true
    target_port                = 3000
    transport                  = "http"

    dynamic "ip_security_restriction" {
      for_each = { for idx, ip in local.front_door_backend_ips : "fd${idx}" => ip }
      content {
        name             = ip_security_restriction.key
        action           = "Allow"
        ip_address_range = ip_security_restriction.value
        description      = "Azure Front Door backend IP"
      }
    }

    traffic_weight {
      latest_revision = true
      percentage      = 100
    }
  }

  template {

    container {

      name   = "lexxi"
      image  = "${var.shared_container_registry_login_server}/${var.container_lexxi_app_image_name}:dev-latest"
      cpu    = var.container_app_cpu
      memory = var.container_app_memory

      # Database credentials from Key Vault
      # Note: Container Apps doesn't support @Microsoft.KeyVault() syntax
      # We fetch actual values from Key Vault via Terraform at apply time
      env {
        name  = "POSTGRES_URL"
        value = azurerm_key_vault_secret.intellxxdna_postgres_url.value
      }

      env {
        name  = "OPENAI_API_KEY"
        value = azurerm_key_vault_secret.lexxi_openai_token.value
      }

      env {
        name  = "CORS_ALLOW_ORIGINS"
        value = azurerm_key_vault_secret.lexxi_cors_allow_origins.value
      }

      env {
        name  = "SENTRY_DSN"
        value = azurerm_key_vault_secret.lexxi_sentry_dsn.value
      }

      env {
        name  = "MLFLOW_TRACKING_URI"
        value = "https://api-dev.ixxdna.com/mlflow/"
      }

      env {
        name  = "MLFLOW_USERNAME"
        value = azurerm_key_vault_secret.mlflow_admin_username.value
      }

      env {
        name  = "MLFLOW_PASSWORD"
        value = azurerm_key_vault_secret.mlflow_admin_password.value
      }

      # Redis configuration from module
      # Note: Redis secrets are already stored in Key Vault by the redis module
      # We reference the module outputs here which come from the Redis resource
      # env {
      #   name  = "REDIS_HOST"
      #   value = module.redis.redis_hostname
      # }
      # 
      # env {
      #   name  = "REDIS_PORT"
      #   value = tostring(module.redis.redis_port)
      # }
      # 
      # env {
      #   name  = "REDIS_PASSWORD"
      #   value = module.redis.redis_primary_access_key
      # }
      # 
      # env {
      #   name  = "REDIS_CONNECTION_STRING"
      #   value = module.redis.redis_primary_connection_string
      # }
    }

    min_replicas = var.container_app_min_replicas
    max_replicas = var.container_app_max_replicas
  }

  registry {
    server               = var.shared_container_registry_login_server
    username             = var.shared_container_registry_username
    password_secret_name = "acr-password"
  }

  secret {
    name  = "acr-password"
    value = var.shared_container_registry_password
  }

  # NOTE: Lexxi container app uses Terraform-fetched values from Key Vault
  # (see env blocks above), so no additional secret blocks needed here

  tags = local.tags

  lifecycle {
    ignore_changes = [workload_profile_name]
  }

  depends_on = [
    azurerm_container_app_environment.env,
    azurerm_postgresql_flexible_server.postgres,
    azurerm_key_vault_secret.postgres_host,
    azurerm_key_vault_secret.postgres_port,
    azurerm_key_vault_secret.lexxi_postgres_db,
    azurerm_key_vault_secret.postgres_user,
    azurerm_key_vault_secret.postgres_password
    # module.redis
  ]
}






##=============================================================================
## KEYCLOAK CONTAINER APP
##=============================================================================
## Keycloak Identity and Access Management (IAM)
## Configuration:
## - Image: keycloak/keycloak:latest (official Docker image)
## - Database: PostgreSQL database "keycloak"
## - Admin: Configured via environment variables
## - Features: OIDC/OAuth2, LDAP/AD integration, realms, clients
## - Access: Public via Front Door + direct
## - Port: 8080
##

##=============================================================================
## KEY VAULT SECRETS - KEYCLOAK CONFIGURATION
##=============================================================================
## Keycloak-specific configuration stored securely in Key Vault
## These values are referenced by the Keycloak container app via Key Vault URIs
##=============================================================================

# Keycloak Database URL (JDBC connection string)
resource "azurerm_key_vault_secret" "keycloak_db_url" {
  name  = "keycloak-db-url"
  value = "jdbc:postgresql://${azurerm_postgresql_flexible_server.postgres.fqdn}/keycloak"

  key_vault_id = module.storage.key_vault_id

  depends_on = [azurerm_postgresql_flexible_server.postgres]
}

# Keycloak Database Username
resource "azurerm_key_vault_secret" "keycloak_db_username" {
  name         = "keycloak-db-username"
  value        = var.postgres_admin_username
  key_vault_id = module.storage.key_vault_id
}

# Keycloak Database Password
resource "azurerm_key_vault_secret" "keycloak_db_password" {
  name         = "keycloak-db-password"
  value        = var.postgres_admin_password
  key_vault_id = module.storage.key_vault_id
}

# Keycloak Admin Username
resource "azurerm_key_vault_secret" "keycloak_admin" {
  name         = "keycloak-admin"
  value        = var.keycloak_admin_username
  key_vault_id = module.storage.key_vault_id
}

# Keycloak Admin Password
resource "azurerm_key_vault_secret" "keycloak_admin_password" {
  name         = "keycloak-admin-password"
  value        = var.keycloak_admin_password
  key_vault_id = module.storage.key_vault_id
}

# Keycloak Container App
resource "azurerm_container_app" "keycloak" {

  name                         = "${local.resource_prefix}-auth"
  container_app_environment_id = azurerm_container_app_environment.env.id
  resource_group_name          = azurerm_resource_group.rg.name
  revision_mode                = "Single"

  # Enable managed identity for Key Vault secret access
  identity {
    type = "SystemAssigned"
  }

  # Enable ingress for public access
  # Note: Multiple revisions allow zero-downtime deployments with traffic splitting
  # GitHub Actions will manage gradual traffic shift from old revision (90%) to new revision (10%)
  ingress {

    allow_insecure_connections = true
    external_enabled           = true
    target_port                = 8080
    transport                  = "http"

    dynamic "ip_security_restriction" {
      for_each = { for idx, ip in local.front_door_backend_ips : "fd${idx}" => ip }
      content {
        name             = ip_security_restriction.key
        action           = "Allow"
        ip_address_range = ip_security_restriction.value
        description      = "Azure Front Door backend IP"
      }
    }

    # B.1 Zero-Downtime Deployment Strategy with Traffic Splitting
    # Allows gradual rollout: 10% new, 90% old → 25/75 → 50/50 → 75/25 → 100% new
    # Deployment script (bin/deploy-keycloak-zero-downtime.sh) dynamically manages traffic weights
    # and updates revision references as new revisions are created by Azure

    traffic_weight {
      latest_revision = true
      percentage      = 100 # Terraform baseline: 100% on latest
      # Deployment script modifies this to 10% for zero-downtime rollout
    }
  }

  template {

    container {

      name   = "keycloak"
      image  = "keycloak/keycloak:latest"
      cpu    = 1.0
      memory = "2Gi"

      # Start Keycloak in production mode
      command = ["/opt/keycloak/bin/kc.sh", "start"]

      # Database configuration - values from Key Vault secrets
      # Note: References the secret block definitions below for Key Vault integration

      # Database type.
      env {
        name  = "KC_DB"
        value = "postgres"
      }

      # Database url, username, and password from Key Vault secrets
      env {
        name  = "KC_DB_URL"
        value = azurerm_key_vault_secret.keycloak_db_url.value
      }

      env {
        name  = "KC_DB_USERNAME"
        value = azurerm_key_vault_secret.keycloak_db_username.value
      }

      env {
        name  = "KC_DB_PASSWORD"
        value = azurerm_key_vault_secret.keycloak_db_password.value
      }

      # Admin credentials - values from Key Vault secrets

      env {
        name  = "KEYCLOAK_ADMIN"
        value = azurerm_key_vault_secret.keycloak_admin.value
      }

      env {
        name  = "KEYCLOAK_ADMIN_PASSWORD"
        value = azurerm_key_vault_secret.keycloak_admin_password.value
      }

      # Note: Keycloak has strict type validation for configuration parameters,
      # so boolean and enum values must be direct values, not Key Vault references

      env {
        name  = "KC_HOSTNAME_STRICT"
        value = "false"
      }

      env {
        name  = "KC_HTTP_ENABLED"
        value = "true"
      }

      env {
        name  = "KC_PROXY"
        value = "edge"
      }

      env {
        name  = "KC_PROXY_HEADERS"
        value = "xforwarded"
      }

      env {
        name  = "KC_SCHEME"
        value = "https"
      }

      env {
        name  = "KC_HTTP_RELATIVE_PATH"
        value = "/auth"
      }

      env {
        name  = "KC_SSL_REQUIRED"
        value = "none"
      }
    }

    min_replicas = 1
    max_replicas = 2
  }

  tags = local.tags

  lifecycle {
    ignore_changes = [workload_profile_name, template, secret]
  }

  depends_on = [
    azurerm_container_app_environment.env,
    azurerm_postgresql_flexible_server_database.keycloak_database,
    azurerm_key_vault_secret.keycloak_db_url,
    azurerm_key_vault_secret.keycloak_db_username,
    azurerm_key_vault_secret.keycloak_db_password,
    azurerm_key_vault_secret.keycloak_admin,
    azurerm_key_vault_secret.keycloak_admin_password
  ]
}

##=============================================================================
## KEY VAULT ACCESS POLICY - KEYCLOAK
##=============================================================================
## Grant Keycloak container app managed identity permissions to read secrets
## from Key Vault for environment variable substitution
## NOTE: This resource is commented out initially and must be applied in a 
## second step after the Keycloak container app (and its identity) is created.
## To apply: Uncomment this block and run terraform apply again.
##=============================================================================

resource "azurerm_key_vault_access_policy" "keycloak" {

  key_vault_id = module.storage.key_vault_id
  tenant_id    = data.azurerm_client_config.current.tenant_id
  object_id    = azurerm_container_app.keycloak.identity[0].principal_id

  secret_permissions = ["Get", "List"]

  depends_on = [azurerm_container_app.keycloak]
}



##=============================================================================
## MLFLOW SERVER
##=============================================================================
## MLflow Tracking Server for ML experiment tracking and model registry
## Configuration:
## - Image: ghcr.io/mlflow/mlflow:latest (official MLflow image)
## - Backend Store: PostgreSQL 'mlflow' database (experiment/run metadata)
## - Artifact Store: Azure Blob Storage container 'mlflow-artifacts'
## - Port: 5000
## - Access: Public via Front Door at /mlflow/*
##

# MLflow dedicated PostgreSQL database
resource "azurerm_postgresql_flexible_server_database" "mlflow_database" {

  name      = "mlflow"
  server_id = azurerm_postgresql_flexible_server.postgres.id
  charset   = "UTF8"
  collation = "en_US.utf8"

  depends_on = [azurerm_postgresql_flexible_server.postgres]
}

# MLflow artifacts blob storage container
resource "azurerm_storage_container" "mlflow_artifacts" {

  name                  = "mlflow-artifacts"
  storage_account_name  = module.storage.storage_account_name
  container_access_type = "private"

  depends_on = [module.storage]
}

# Data source to read the existing storage account's primary connection string
data "azurerm_storage_account" "storage" {

  name                = module.storage.storage_account_name
  resource_group_name = azurerm_resource_group.rg.name
}

# MLflow Flask secret key for CSRF protection
resource "azurerm_key_vault_secret" "mlflow_flask_secret_key" {

  name         = "mlflow-flask-secret-key"
  value        = var.mlflow_flask_secret_key
  key_vault_id = module.storage.key_vault_id

  depends_on = [module.storage]
}

# MLflow admin username
resource "azurerm_key_vault_secret" "mlflow_admin_username" {

  name         = "mlflow-admin-username"
  value        = var.mlflow_admin_username
  key_vault_id = module.storage.key_vault_id

  depends_on = [module.storage]
}

# MLflow admin password
resource "azurerm_key_vault_secret" "mlflow_admin_password" {

  name         = "mlflow-admin-password"
  value        = var.mlflow_admin_password
  key_vault_id = module.storage.key_vault_id

  depends_on = [module.storage]
}

# MLflow backend store URI (PostgreSQL SQLAlchemy connection string)
resource "azurerm_key_vault_secret" "mlflow_backend_store_uri" {

  name         = "mlflow-backend-store-uri"
  value        = "postgresql://${var.intellxxdna_admin_username}:${urlencode(var.intellxxdna_admin_password)}@${azurerm_postgresql_flexible_server.postgres.fqdn}:5432/mlflow?options=-csearch_path%3Dpublic"
  key_vault_id = module.storage.key_vault_id

  depends_on = [azurerm_postgresql_flexible_server_database.mlflow_database]
}

# MLflow artifact root URI (Azure Blob Storage wasbs:// scheme)
resource "azurerm_key_vault_secret" "mlflow_artifact_root" {

  name         = "mlflow-artifact-root"
  value        = "wasbs://mlflow-artifacts@${module.storage.storage_account_name}.blob.core.windows.net/"
  key_vault_id = module.storage.key_vault_id

  depends_on = [azurerm_storage_container.mlflow_artifacts]
}

# MLflow storage connection string for Azure Blob artifact access
resource "azurerm_key_vault_secret" "mlflow_storage_connection_string" {

  name         = "mlflow-storage-connection-string"
  value        = data.azurerm_storage_account.storage.primary_connection_string
  key_vault_id = module.storage.key_vault_id

  depends_on = [data.azurerm_storage_account.storage]
}

# MLflow Container App
resource "azurerm_container_app" "mlflow" {

  name                         = "${local.resource_prefix}-mlflow"
  container_app_environment_id = azurerm_container_app_environment.env.id
  resource_group_name          = azurerm_resource_group.rg.name
  revision_mode                = "Single"

  identity {
    type = "SystemAssigned"
  }

  ingress {

    allow_insecure_connections = true
    external_enabled           = true
    target_port                = 5000
    transport                  = "http"

    dynamic "ip_security_restriction" {
      for_each = { for idx, ip in local.front_door_backend_ips : "fd${idx}" => ip }
      content {
        name             = ip_security_restriction.key
        action           = "Allow"
        ip_address_range = ip_security_restriction.value
        description      = "Azure Front Door backend IP"
      }
    }

    traffic_weight {
      latest_revision = true
      percentage      = 100
    }
  }

  template {

    container {

      name   = "mlflow"
      image  = "ixdnaacrshared.azurecr.io/mlflow-azure:latest"
      cpu    = 1.0
      memory = "2Gi"

      # azure-storage-blob is pre-installed in the custom image (ixdnaacrshared.azurecr.io/mlflow-azure:latest)
      # built from: FROM ghcr.io/mlflow/mlflow:latest + RUN pip install azure-storage-blob
      # Basic auth enabled via --app-name basic-auth (MLflow 2.5+)
      # Admin credentials written to config file at startup from Key Vault env vars
      command = [
        "/bin/sh", "-c",
        "pip install 'mlflow[auth]' && printf '[mlflow]\\ndefault_permission = NO_PERMISSIONS\\ndatabase_uri = sqlite:////tmp/auth.db\\nadmin_username = %s\\nadmin_password = %s\\n' \"$MLFLOW_ADMIN_USERNAME\" \"$MLFLOW_ADMIN_PASSWORD\" > /tmp/basic_auth.ini && MLFLOW_AUTH_CONFIG_PATH=/tmp/basic_auth.ini mlflow server --app-name basic-auth --backend-store-uri $MLFLOW_BACKEND_STORE_URI --artifacts-destination $MLFLOW_ARTIFACT_ROOT --host 0.0.0.0 --port 5000 --allowed-hosts ${local.resource_prefix}-mlflow.${azurerm_container_app_environment.env.default_domain},api-dev.ixxdna.com --cors-allowed-origins https://api-dev.ixxdna.com"
      ]

      env {
        name  = "MLFLOW_BACKEND_STORE_URI"
        value = azurerm_key_vault_secret.mlflow_backend_store_uri.value
      }

      env {
        name  = "MLFLOW_ARTIFACT_ROOT"
        value = azurerm_key_vault_secret.mlflow_artifact_root.value
      }

      env {
        name  = "AZURE_STORAGE_CONNECTION_STRING"
        value = data.azurerm_storage_account.storage.primary_connection_string
      }

      env {
        name  = "MLFLOW_ADMIN_USERNAME"
        value = azurerm_key_vault_secret.mlflow_admin_username.value
      }

      env {
        name  = "MLFLOW_ADMIN_PASSWORD"
        value = azurerm_key_vault_secret.mlflow_admin_password.value
      }

      env {
        name  = "MLFLOW_FLASK_SERVER_SECRET_KEY"
        value = azurerm_key_vault_secret.mlflow_flask_secret_key.value
      }

      env {
        name  = "MLFLOW_ALLOWED_ORIGINS"
        value = "https://api-dev.ixxdna.com"
      }

    }

    min_replicas = 1
    max_replicas = 1
  }

  registry {
    server               = var.shared_container_registry_login_server
    username             = var.shared_container_registry_username
    password_secret_name = "acr-password"
  }

  secret {
    name  = "acr-password"
    value = var.shared_container_registry_password
  }

  tags = local.tags

  lifecycle {
    ignore_changes = [workload_profile_name]
  }

  depends_on = [
    azurerm_container_app_environment.env,
    azurerm_postgresql_flexible_server_database.mlflow_database,
    azurerm_storage_container.mlflow_artifacts,
    azurerm_key_vault_secret.mlflow_backend_store_uri,
    azurerm_key_vault_secret.mlflow_artifact_root,
    azurerm_key_vault_secret.mlflow_storage_connection_string
  ]
}

# Key Vault access policy for MLflow managed identity
resource "azurerm_key_vault_access_policy" "mlflow" {

  key_vault_id = module.storage.key_vault_id
  tenant_id    = data.azurerm_client_config.current.tenant_id
  object_id    = azurerm_container_app.mlflow.identity[0].principal_id

  secret_permissions = ["Get", "List"]

  depends_on = [azurerm_container_app.mlflow]
}



##=============================================================================
## APPLICATION GATEWAY MODULE
##=============================================================================
## Public gateway with WAF for secure access to Container App
## Configuration:
## - SKU: Standard_v2 (cost-optimized)
## - WAF: Enabled in Detection mode (logs threats, doesn't block)
## - Listener: HTTP -> HTTPS redirect
## - Backend: Container App (internal FQDN)
## - Certificate: Self-signed for development
##
## Access:
## - Public IP: <ip_address>
## - URL: https://<ip_address> (self-signed cert warning expected)
## - HTTP automatically redirects to HTTPS

##=============================================================================
## AZURE FRONT DOOR MODULE
##=============================================================================
## Provides global public access with managed HTTPS (*.azurefd.net certificate)
## Features:
## - Automatic HTTPS certificate management (no renewal needed)
## - WAF protection with OWASP ModSecurity rules
## - Global CDN with DDoS protection (layers 3-7)
## - HTTP → HTTPS automatic redirect
## - Health probes for backend monitoring
## Status: Replaces Application Gateway for lower cost ($6/month vs $32/month) and simplicity
##

module "front_door" {

  source = "../../modules/front-door"

  resource_group_name = azurerm_resource_group.rg.name
  environment         = var.environment
  resource_prefix     = local.resource_prefix
  tags                = local.tags

  # Front Door Configuration
  front_door_sku          = var.front_door_sku
  custom_domain_host_name = "api-dev.ixxdna.com"

  # Backend Configuration - Multiple backends for Lexxi, RD, and Keycloak with path-based routing
  # NOTE: Route Keycloak over HTTPS to bypass HTTP-only proxy header issues
  backends = [
    {
      name              = "lexxi"
      fqdn              = azurerm_container_app.lexxi.ingress[0].fqdn
      port              = 80
      protocol          = "Http"
      path              = "/lexxi/*"
      health_path       = "/"
      strip_path_prefix = true
      strip_order       = 1
    },
    {
      name              = "rd"
      fqdn              = azurerm_container_app.rd.ingress[0].fqdn
      port              = 80
      protocol          = "Http"
      path              = "/rd/*"
      health_path       = "/"
      strip_path_prefix = true
      strip_order       = 3
    },
    {
      name             = "keycloak"
      fqdn             = azurerm_container_app.keycloak.ingress[0].fqdn
      port             = 443
      protocol         = "Https"
      path             = "/auth/*"
      health_path      = "/auth/realms/master/.well-known/openid-configuration"
      session_affinity = true
    },
    {
      name              = "mlflow"
      fqdn              = azurerm_container_app.mlflow.ingress[0].fqdn
      port              = 80
      protocol          = "Http"
      path              = "/mlflow/*"
      health_path       = "/"
      strip_path_prefix = true
      strip_order       = 2
    }
  ]

  # Diagnostics
  log_analytics_workspace_id = module.storage.log_analytics_workspace_id

  # WAF Configuration
  enable_waf = var.enable_front_door_waf
  waf_mode   = var.front_door_waf_mode

  depends_on = [
    azurerm_container_app.lexxi,
    azurerm_container_app.rd,
    azurerm_container_app.keycloak,
    azurerm_container_app.mlflow,
    module.networking
  ]
}



##=============================================================================
## STATIC WEB APP MODULE (lexxi)
##=============================================================================
## Azure Static Web App for Angular frontend deployment
## Features:
## - Free tier (no cost)
## - GitHub Actions CI/CD integration
## - Managed HTTPS certificate (*.azurestaticapps.net)
## - Global CDN distribution
## - API proxy to backend via staticwebapp.config.json
## 
## Deployment:
## - Automatically deployed from develop branch
## - Build command: ng build
## - Build output: dist/
## - GitHub Enterprise authentication via PAT token
##
## Access:
## - Frontend: https://<app-name>.azurestaticapps.net
## - API: Proxied through Static Web App to Front Door endpoint
##

module "static_web_app_lexxi" {

  source = "../../modules/static-web-app"

  resource_group_name = azurerm_resource_group.rg.name
  location            = var.azure_region
  environment         = var.environment
  resource_prefix     = local.resource_prefix
  app_name            = "lexxis"

  # GitHub Configuration
  github_repo_url = var.lexxi_github_repo_url
  github_token    = var.github_token
  github_branch   = var.lexxi_github_branch

  # Build Configuration
  app_location    = var.swa_app_location
  output_location = var.swa_output_location
  build_command   = var.swa_build_command

  # API Configuration
  api_endpoint = module.front_door.endpoint_fqdn
  api_key      = ""

  sku_tier = "Free"
  sku_size = "Free"
  tags     = local.tags

  depends_on = [
    module.front_door,
    azurerm_container_app.lexxi
  ]
}

## STATIC WEB APP CUSTOM DOMAIN for lexxi
resource "azurerm_static_web_app_custom_domain" "lexxi_dev" {

  count             = var.enable_custom_domain_to_lexxi ? 1 : 0
  static_web_app_id = module.static_web_app_lexxi.static_web_app_id
  domain_name       = "lexxi-az-dev.ixxdna.com"
  validation_type   = "cname-delegation"
}



##=============================================================================
## STATIC WEB APP MODULE (rd)
##=============================================================================
## Azure Static Web App for Angular frontend deployment
## Features:
## - Free tier (no cost)
## - GitHub Actions CI/CD integration
## - Managed HTTPS certificate (*.azurestaticapps.net)
## - Global CDN distribution
## - API proxy to backend via staticwebapp.config.json
## 
## Deployment:
## - Automatically deployed from develop branch
## - Build command: ng build
## - Build output: dist/
## - GitHub Enterprise authentication via PAT token
##
## Access:
## - Frontend: https://<app-name>.azurestaticapps.net
## - API: Proxied through Static Web App to Front Door endpoint
##

module "static_web_app_rd" {

  source = "../../modules/static-web-app"

  resource_group_name = azurerm_resource_group.rg.name
  location            = var.azure_region
  environment         = var.environment
  resource_prefix     = local.resource_prefix
  app_name            = "rd"

  # GitHub Configuration
  github_repo_url = var.rd_github_repo_url
  github_token    = var.github_token
  github_branch   = var.rd_github_branch

  # Build Configuration
  app_location    = var.swa_app_location
  output_location = var.swa_output_location
  build_command   = var.swa_build_command

  # API Configuration
  api_endpoint = module.front_door.endpoint_fqdn
  api_key      = ""

  sku_tier = "Free"
  sku_size = "Free"
  tags     = local.tags

  depends_on = [
    module.front_door,
    azurerm_container_app.rd
  ]
}

## STATIC WEB APP CUSTOM DOMAIN for rd
resource "azurerm_static_web_app_custom_domain" "rd_dev" {

  count             = var.enable_custom_domain_to_rd ? 1 : 0
  static_web_app_id = module.static_web_app_rd.static_web_app_id
  domain_name       = "data-cleansing-az-dev.ixxdna.com"
  validation_type   = "cname-delegation"
}

## STATIC WEB APP CUSTOM DOMAIN for rd
resource "azurerm_static_web_app_custom_domain" "rnd_dev" {

  count             = var.enable_custom_domain_to_rd ? 1 : 0
  static_web_app_id = module.static_web_app_rd.static_web_app_id
  domain_name       = "rnd-dev.ixxdna.com"
  validation_type   = "cname-delegation"
}

content.text:
Now deploy:

```bash
terraform apply -target=azurerm_container_app.lexxi
```

**Why this approach:** The MLflow admin credentials are already in Key Vault (created for the MLflow container), so the Lexxi container reuses them directly — no new secrets needed. The tracking URI is hardcoded since it's just a URL, consistent with how `CORS_ALLOW_ORIGINS` is handled elsewhere in the file.

message.content: i don't want to touch more this repository /opt/development/git/shokworks/intellxxdna/terraform/intell-terraform-infra-azure/ from this session

content.text: Understood, I won't touch that repository anymore.

message.content: how can I set debug true?

content.text:
In your `.env`:

```env
DEBUG=true
```

That's already the default (`debug: bool = Field(default=True)` in `GeneralSettings`), so it should already be active locally. When `DEBUG=true` the logging level is set to `DEBUG` and you'll see the `[mlflow]` messages in the console.

message.content: but I need to deploy again... is there a way to do it externally?

content.thinking:
The user wants to set DEBUG=true in the deployed environment without touching the Terraform repo. Let me think about options:

1. **Azure Portal** - manually set the env var in the Container App via Azure Portal
2. **Azure CLI** - update the container app env var directly via `az containerapp update`
3. **GitHub Actions / CI CD** - trigger a redeploy with the env var

Since they don't want to touch the Terraform repo, the Azure CLI approach would be the most direct. Let me suggest using `az containerapp update` to add/update an environment variable.

Actually, looking at the config.py, `DEBUG` is read by `GeneralSettings` which has `env_file = ".env"` and `case_sensitive = False`. 

Since it's a pydantic-settings field, it reads from environment variables directly too. So I can set `DEBUG=true` as an environment variable in the Azure Container App without Terraform. I'd use the Azure CLI to update the container app with the new environment variable, though I'd need the exact container app name and resource group from the Terraform outputs to construct the command properly.

Looking at the MLflow logs, the container app name follows the pattern `intellxxdna-lexxi-dev-lexxi`, so that's what I'd target. But given that the user mentioned not wanting to touch the repository further, they might prefer avoiding Azure CLI commands too. The DEBUG flag ultimately controls the log level, so I could suggest a simpler approach that doesn't require additional tooling or configuration changes.

content.text:
Two options without touching Terraform:

**Option 1 — Azure CLI (fastest):**
```bash
az containerapp update \
  --name intellxxdna-lexxi-dev-lexxi \
  --resource-group <your-resource-group> \
  --set-env-vars DEBUG=true
```

**Option 2 — Azure Portal (no CLI needed):**
1. Open the Azure Portal → **Container Apps** → `intellxxdna-lexxi-dev-lexxi`
2. Left menu → **Containers** → **Environment variables**
3. Add `DEBUG` = `true`
4. Click **Save** — it creates a new revision automatically

Both approaches override the env var at runtime without modifying Terraform or redeploying the image. When you're done debugging, just remove it or set it back to `false` the same way.

        You are the **Lexxi Concerns Extractor**, a clinical decision-support
        assistant for the IntellxxDNA genomics platform.

        Your job: help a clinician identify the most relevant patient concerns
        from their input so the downstream Rules Engine can select and rank SNPs.

        ———————————————————————————————————————————————————————————
        CONVERSATION FLOW  (follow this exactly)
        ———————————————————————————————————————————————————————————

        **STEP 0 — Greetings, small talk, or off-topic messages.**

        If the clinician sends a greeting (e.g. "hi", "hello", "good morning"),
        a general question, or anything that does NOT contain patient concerns
        or medical information:
        → Respond warmly and naturally, then guide them to provide patient info.
          Example: "Hello! I'm Lexxi, ready to help you identify your patient's
          key concerns. Please share your patient's main health concerns to get
          started."
        → Do NOT call any tools. Do NOT produce structured output.
        → Do NOT jump to the follow-up question about family history.
        → Wait for the clinician to provide actual patient information.

        **STEP 1 — Clinician provides patient concerns.**

        A)  If the message includes an ATTACHED FILE (look for "[ATTACHED FILE:"
            markers in the message), PDF content, image, document content,
            OR already mentions family history AND medications/supplements:
            → Treat all information as sufficient.
            → Do NOT ask for more.  Go directly to → ANALYSIS.
            → Make sure to tag concerns derived from uploaded documents with
              the appropriate tags ("Family History" for family history docs,
              "Genomic Results" for genomic/lab reports, etc.)

        B)  If the message contains patient concerns but NO attached file
            and NO mention of family history, medications, or supplements:
            → Go directly to → ANALYSIS using the provided concerns.

        **STEP 2+ — Clinician provides additional info in follow-up messages.**

        • If they provide additional info (text, files, or both):
          - Acknowledge briefly (one sentence).
          - Incorporate everything into your analysis, including any
            previously uploaded file content visible in the conversation.
          - Go to → ANALYSIS.

        ———————————————————————————————————————————————————————————
        ANALYSIS  (when you have enough information)
        ———————————————————————————————————————————————————————————

        1. Identify candidate concerns by matching the clinician's input
           against AVAILABLE_CONCERNS. Use the SYNONYM MAPPINGS below to
           resolve medical terms, abbreviations, and colloquial names to
           the correct official concern name.
           When analyzing uploaded documents, extract ALL relevant medical
           conditions, family history conditions, and medications/supplements.
           Examples: "high blood pressure" → "Hypertension",
           "breast cancer" → "Cancer Prevention",
           "father had heart disease" → "Cardiac" (tagged "Family History"),
           "mother had diabetes" → "Diabetes Type 2" (tagged "Family History").

        2. Call the `extract_concerns` tool with your validated list.
           Always use the EXACT official concern name from AVAILABLE_CONCERNS.

        3. After the tool returns, write a brief conversational response
           to the clinician that:

           a) Starts with a context-specific sentence like:
              "Based on [what was shared] combined with Lexxi's findings
              from their genomics, below are suggested concerns to explore
              further with your patient."

           b) Then includes this standard guidance:
              "Once you add the patient's health concerns, click the prompt
              below to automatically populate the middle section with the
              most relevant SNPs, pre-selected in each concern card. You
              can expand a card to explore additional SNPs by clicking
              See more relevant SNPs, or remove any SNPs as needed.
              Based on your selection, a SNPs Not to Miss group may appear
              for consideration during this or a future consult."

           c) Do NOT repeat the JSON or list the concerns in text form.
              The structured output from the tool is what the UI will render.

        ———————————————————————————————————————————————————————————
        STRICT RULES
        ———————————————————————————————————————————————————————————

        1. You may ONLY use concern names from AVAILABLE_CONCERNS.
        2. Do NOT invent, modify, or generalize concern names.
    
    3. If no concerns match, pass an empty list to the tool.
        4. Each concern needs at least one tag.
        5. If "longevity" is mentioned, consider expanding to related
           concerns (Cardiac, Diabetes Type 2, Inflammation,
           Nutrient Deficiencies, Cognition) per Lexxi protocol.
        6. If files are provided in the FIRST message, do NOT ask for more.
        7. When responding to greetings (Step 0): respond ONLY with plain
           text. Do NOT call any tools.

content.text:
Actually the data **is** being sent — look at these two lines in your logs:

```
POST /mlflow/api/2.0/mlflow/runs/create HTTP/1.1" 200 888
POST /mlflow/api/2.0/mlflow/runs/log-batch HTTP/1.1" 200 2
```

Both return `200` — the run was created and the params/metrics were logged successfully.

The reason you can't see it in the UI is still the **CORS issue** — the browser gets a `403` on `runs/search` so the UI shows nothing. That's the `--cors-allowed-origins` Terraform change we already made but hasn't been deployed yet.

You can confirm the data is there right now by querying directly, bypassing the browser:

```bash
curl -u admin:lGo6iH10MavP \
  "https://api-dev.ixxdna.com/mlflow/api/2.0/mlflow/runs/search" \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"experiment_ids": ["1"], "max_results": 5}'
```

You should see runs with params and metrics in the JSON response. Once the `--cors-allowed-origins` deployment goes through, the UI will show them too.

message.content: how can i check if this --cors-allowed-origins is correct?

content.thinking:
The user wants to check if `--cors-allowed-origins` is correctly set on the MLflow server. Since the MLflow server is running in the Azure Container App, they can check via the container logs or by exec-ing into the container to see the actual startup command being used.

Or they can check via the Azure CLI to see the current environment variables and command.

Or they can check via curl with an OPTIONS preflight request to see if the CORS headers are returned.

The most straightforward way is to send a CORS preflight request (OPTIONS) to the MLflow API and check if the `Access-Control-Allow-Origin` header is returned.

content.text:
Send a CORS preflight request — if `--cors-allowed-origins` is working, the response will include `Access-Control-Allow-Origin`:

```bash
curl -si -X OPTIONS "https://api-dev.ixxdna.com/mlflow/api/2.0/mlflow/runs/search" \
  -H "Origin: https://api-dev.ixxdna.com" \
  -H "Access-Control-Request-Method: POST" \
  -H "Access-Control-Request-Headers: Content-Type" \
  | grep -i "access-control"
```

**If working**, you'll see:
```
access-control-allow-origin: https://api-dev.ixxdna.com
access-control-allow-methods: ...
```

**If still broken**, the headers will be missing entirely.

You can also verify the startup command actually includes the flag by checking the running container:

```bash
az containerapp exec \
  --name intellxxdna-lexxi-dev-mlflow \
  --resource-group <your-resource-group> \
  --command "cat /proc/1/cmdline | tr '\0' ' '"
```

That prints the exact command the MLflow process started with — you should see `--cors-allowed-origins https://api-dev.ixxdna.com` in there.

message.content: can I see it n teh logs?

content.text:
Yes, look for the startup line in the container logs right after a deployment/restart. MLflow prints its full command when it starts:

```
[...] INFO mlflow.server: Starting gunicorn server...
```

Or search for the specific flag:

```bash
az containerapp logs show \
  --name intellxxdna-lexxi-dev-mlflow \
  --resource-group <your-resource-group> \
  --tail 50 \
  | grep -i "cors\|allowed-origins\|gunicorn\|Starting"
```

If the flag is active you'll see something like:

```
... mlflow server --app-name basic-auth ... --cors-allowed-origins https://api-dev.ixxdna.com
```

If that line isn't there, the container hasn't restarted with the new Terraform config yet — meaning `terraform apply` hasn't been run or the new revision hasn't taken effect.

content.thinking:
The logs are very clear now. Everything is working perfectly. I can see:

1. `POST /mlflow/api/2.0/mlflow/runs/create HTTP/1.1" 200 888` - run created
2. `POST /mlflow/api/2.0/mlflow/runs/log-batch HTTP/1.1" 200 2` - batch logged
3. `GET /mlflow/api/2.0/mlflow/runs/get?run_uuid=9f5042c013d04a80a96495b190c2a201` - run retrieved


4. `POST /mlflow/api/2.0/mlflow/runs/update HTTP/1.1" 200 421` - run updated and completed

And most importantly, MLflow is displaying the run URL directly, showing the experiment and run are accessible in the UI.

Everything is working as expected—the tracking is functioning properly. There are a couple of minor noise issues like the git warning since git isn't installed in the container, which could be quieted with an environment variable, but these don't affect the core functionality.

content.text:
**Everything is working.** MLflow itself printed the run URL in the logs:

```
🏃 View run concerns_extractor at: https://api-dev.ixxdna.com/mlflow/#/experiments/1/runs/9f5042c013d04a80a96495b190c2a201
🧪 View experiment at: https://api-dev.ixxdna.com/mlflow/#/experiments/1
```

Open that URL directly — you'll see the run with all params and metrics.

---

The only noise worth silencing is the `git` warning. MLflow tries to capture the git SHA but `git` isn't installed in the container. Add this env var to silence it (Azure Portal or CLI):

```env
GIT_PYTHON_REFRESH=quiet
```

That's it — tracking is fully functional.

The MLflow server is receiving and processing everything correctly — all `200 OK`. The full flow is confirmed end-to-end:

| Time | Call | Status |
|------|------|--------|
| 20:00:54 | `runs/create` | ✅ 200 |
| 20:00:54 | `runs/log-batch` (params) | ✅ 200 |
| 20:01:08 | `runs/log-batch` (metrics) | ✅ 200 |
| 20:01:08 | `runs/get` | ✅ 200 |
| 20:01:09 | `runs/update` (FINISHED) | ✅ 200 |

**Everything is working perfectly.** The run is stored in the database. Just open the URL from the Lexxi logs and you'll see it:

```
https://api-dev.ixxdna.com/mlflow/#/experiments/1/runs/9f5042c013d04a80a96495b190c2a201
```

content.text:
I now have enough information from all fetched pages to give a thorough, accurate answer. Here is the synthesis:

---

## MLflow Tracing: Key Technical Details

### 1. Auto-Tracing with `mlflow.openai.autolog()`

Enabling auto-tracing is a one-liner:

```python
import mlflow
mlflow.openai.autolog()
```

This instruments all OpenAI API calls automatically. Multiple auto-tracing integrations can be active simultaneously (e.g., `mlflow.langchain.autolog()` and `mlflow.openai.autolog()` together).

**What is captured automatically:** function inputs/outputs, execution time, latency, token usage at each step, and various quality metrics — all without modifying application code.

---

### 2. OpenAI Agents SDK Support

**Yes, there is explicit support.** The integrations page lists **"OpenAI Agent"** under *Agent Frameworks (Python)* as one of 40+ supported integrations. The exact invocation is likely `mlflow.openai.autolog()` (the same call used for raw OpenAI), though the dedicated integration page returned 403 errors during fetching. It is listed alongside LangChain, LangGraph, DSPy, CrewAI, PydanticAI, AutoGen, and others.

---

### 3. Manual Tracing APIs

#### `@mlflow.trace` decorator

```python
@mlflow.trace(span_type="func", attributes={"key": "value"})
def my_function(x, y):
    return x + y
```

- Automatically captures: function name, inputs, outputs, execution time, exceptions (as span events), and parent-child span relationships.
- Supported function types:
  - Synchronous functions
  - **Async functions** (v2.16.0+)
  - **Generators** (v2.20.2+)
  - **Async generators** (v2.20.2+)
- Decorator ordering: place `@mlflow.trace` as the **outermost** decorator for custom decorators; for Flask/FastAPI route decorators, place it **inside** (framework decorator outermost).

**Function wrapping** (for third-party code you can't modify):

```python
traced_pow = mlflow.trace(math.pow)
result = traced_pow(x, exp)
```

#### `mlflow.start_span()` — code-block context manager

```python
with mlflow.start_span(name="my_span") as span:
    span.set_inputs({"x": 1, "y": 2})
    result = x + y
    span.set_outputs(result)
```

- Automatically captures: parent-child relationships, exceptions, execution time.
- You must **manually** set inputs, outputs, and the span name.
- Use this for fine-grained control over arbitrary code blocks (not full functions).

#### `mlflow.get_current_active_span()` — dynamic attribute injection

```python
@mlflow.trace(span_type=SpanType.LLM)
def invoke(prompt: str):
    span = mlflow.get_current_active_span()
    span.set_attributes({"model": "gpt-4o-mini"})
    return client.invoke(...)
```

#### `start_active_span`

The documentation does not expose a standalone `mlflow.start_active_span()` top-level function. The active-span concept is accessed via `mlflow.get_current_active_span()` inside a decorated function or `start_span` block. The `mlflow.tracing.context()` utility can inject metadata into the current active trace scope without creating a wrapper span.

---

### 4. Data Captured in Traces

**TraceInfo** (lightweight metadata):
- `trace_id`
- `request_time` (milliseconds epoch)
- `state`: `OK | ERROR | IN_PROGRESS | STATE_UNSPECIFIED`
- `execution_duration` (ms)
- `request_preview` / `response_preview` (JSON-encoded, customizable)
- `tags` (mutable key-value pairs for grouping/querying)

**TraceData** (span tree):
- Spans in a parent-child tree (e.g., root `agent` span → children: LLM call spans, tool call spans)
- Each span: name, type (`SpanType.LLM`, `CHAIN`, `TOOL`, `FUNC`, etc.), inputs, outputs, attributes, execution time, exception events
- Custom attributes: `span.set_attributes({"model": "gpt-4o-mini", ...})`
- Custom trace-level tags: `mlflow.update_current_trace(tags={"env": "prod"})`

**Metrics auto-captured** (especially with auto-tracing): latency per step, token usage, quality metrics.

---

### 5. Tracing and `mlflow.start_run()` Interaction

The documentation does **not** document a direct, explicit API binding between `mlflow.start_run()` and tracing. The two systems are largely independent in their documentation:

- Traces are associated with **experiments** (set via `mlflow.set_experiment("my-experiment")`), not necessarily with runs.
- `mlflow.tracing.context()` is the mechanism for injecting metadata into traces from surrounding code — this is likely how you'd attach run context if needed.
- The `mlflow.get_active_trace_id()` function exists to retrieve the current trace ID, which could be logged to a run manually.

In practice, both can coexist in the same code: set an experiment, start a run for parameter/metric logging, and let traces record LLM call details within the same experiment. However, **automatic association of a trace to an active run is not documented**.

---

### 6. Async Support

Full async support exists at multiple levels:

- `@mlflow.trace` works with `async def` functions natively (since v2.16.0).
- `ContextVar` is **automatically copied to async tasks**, so there is no manual context propagation needed for async/await — unlike multi-threaded code (which requires `contextvars.copy_context()`).
- Async generators supported since v2.20.2.
- **Async trace logging** (background I/O): disabled by default for OSS MLflow; enable with `MLFLOW_ENABLE_ASYNC_TRACE_LOGGING=true`. Configurable with:
  - `MLFLOW_ASYNC_TRACE_LOGGING_MAX_WORKERS` (default: 10)
  - `MLFLOW_ASYNC_TRACE_LOGGING_MAX_QUEUE_SIZE` (default: 1000)
  - `MLFLOW_ASYNC_TRACE_LOGGING_RETRY_TIMEOUT` (default: 500s)

---

### Summary Table

| Feature | API | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| OpenAI auto-trace | `mlflow.openai.autolog()` | One-liner, captures tokens/latency |
| OpenAI Agents SDK | `mlflow.openai.autolog()` (presumed) | Listed as "OpenAI Agent" in 40+ integrations |

content.text:
I now have enough information from all fetched pages to give a thorough, accurate answer. Here is the synthesis:

---

## MLflow Tracing: Key Technical Details

### 1. Auto-Tracing with `mlflow.openai.autolog()`

Enabling auto-tracing is a one-liner:

```python
import mlflow
mlflow.openai.autolog()
```

This instruments all OpenAI API calls automatically. Multiple auto-tracing integrations can be active simultaneously (e.g., `mlflow.langchain.autolog()` and `mlflow.openai.autolog()` together).

**What is captured automatically:** function inputs/outputs, execution time, latency, token usage at each step, and various quality metrics — all without modifying application code.

---

### 2. OpenAI Agents SDK Support

**Yes, there is explicit support.** The integrations page lists **"OpenAI Agent"** under *Agent Frameworks (Python)* as one of 40+ supported integrations. The exact invocation is likely `mlflow.openai.autolog()` (the same call used for raw OpenAI), though the dedicated integration page returned 403 errors during fetching. It is listed alongside LangChain, LangGraph, DSPy, CrewAI, PydanticAI, AutoGen, and others.

---

### 3. Manual Tracing APIs

#### `@mlflow.trace` decorator

```python
@mlflow.trace(span_type="func", attributes={"key": "value"})
def my_function(x, y):
    return x + y
```

- Automatically captures: function name, inputs, outputs, execution time, exceptions (as span events), and parent-child span relationships.
- Supported function types:
  - Synchronous functions
  - **Async functions** (v2.16.0+)
  - **Generators** (v2.20.2+)
  - **Async generators** (v2.20.2+)
- Decorator ordering: place `@mlflow.trace` as the **outermost** decorator for custom decorators; for Flask/FastAPI route decorators, place it **inside** (framework decorator outermost).

**Function wrapping** (for third-party code you can't modify):

```python
traced_pow = mlflow.trace(math.pow)
result = traced_pow(x, exp)
```

#### `mlflow.start_span()` — code-block context manager

```python
with mlflow.start_span(name="my_span") as span:
    span.set_inputs({"x": 1, "y": 2})
    result = x + y
    span.set_outputs(result)
```

- Automatically captures: parent-child relationships, exceptions, execution time.
- You must **manually** set inputs, outputs, and the span name.
- Use this for fine-grained control over arbitrary code blocks (not full functions).

#### `mlflow.get_current_active_span()` — dynamic attribute injection

```python
@mlflow.trace(span_type=SpanType.LLM)
def invoke(prompt: str):
    span = mlflow.get_current_active_span()
    span.set_attributes({"model": "gpt-4o-mini"})
    return client.invoke(...)
```

#### `start_active_span`

The documentation does not expose a standalone `mlflow.start_active_span()` top-level function. The active-span concept is accessed via `mlflow.get_current_active_span()` inside a decorated function or `start_span` block. The `mlflow.tracing.context()` utility can inject metadata into the current active trace scope without creating a wrapper span.

---

### 4. Data Captured in Traces

**TraceInfo** (lightweight metadata):
- `trace_id`
- `request_time` (milliseconds epoch)
- `state`: `OK | ERROR | IN_PROGRESS | STATE_UNSPECIFIED`
- `execution_duration` (ms)
- `request_preview` / `response_preview` (JSON-encoded, customizable)
- `tags` (mutable key-value pairs for grouping/querying)

**TraceData** (span tree):
- Spans in a parent-child tree (e.g., root `agent` span → children: LLM call spans, tool call spans)
- Each span: name, type (`SpanType.LLM`, `CHAIN`, `TOOL`, `FUNC`, etc.), inputs, outputs, attributes, execution time, exception events
- Custom attributes: `span.set_attributes({"model": "gpt-4o-mini", ...})`
- Custom trace-level tags: `mlflow.update_current_trace(tags={"env": "prod"})`

**Metrics auto-captured** (especially with auto-tracing): latency per step, token usage, quality metrics.

---

### 5. Tracing and `mlflow.start_run()` Interaction

The documentation does **not** document a direct, explicit API binding between `mlflow.start_run()` and tracing. The two systems are largely independent in their documentation:

- Traces are associated with **experiments** (set via `mlflow.set_experiment("my-experiment")`), not necessarily with runs.
- `mlflow.tracing.context()` is the mechanism for injecting metadata into traces from surrounding code — this is likely how you'd attach run context if needed.
- The `mlflow.get_active_trace_id()` function exists to retrieve the current trace ID, which could be logged to a run manually.

In practice, both can coexist in the same code: set an experiment, start a run for parameter/metric logging, and let traces record LLM call details within the same experiment. However, **automatic association of a trace to an active run is not documented**.

---

### 6. Async Support

Full async support exists at multiple levels:

- `@mlflow.trace` works with `async def` functions natively (since v2.16.0).
- `ContextVar` is **automatically copied to async tasks**, so there is no manual context propagation needed for async/await — unlike multi-threaded code (which requires `contextvars.copy_context()`).
- Async generators supported since v2.20.2.
- **Async trace logging** (background I/O): disabled by default for OSS MLflow; enable with `MLFLOW_ENABLE_ASYNC_TRACE_LOGGING=true`. Configurable with:
  - `MLFLOW_ASYNC_TRACE_LOGGING_MAX_WORKERS` (default: 10)
  - `MLFLOW_ASYNC_TRACE_LOGGING_MAX_QUEUE_SIZE` (default: 1000)
  - `MLFLOW_ASYNC_TRACE_LOGGING_RETRY_TIMEOUT` (default: 500s)

---

### Summary Table

| Feature | API | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| OpenAI auto-trace | `mlflow.openai.autolog()` | One-liner, captures tokens/latency |
| OpenAI Agents SDK | `mlflow.openai.autolog()` (presumed) | Listed as "OpenAI Agent" in 40+ integrations |
| Function decorator | `@mlflow.trace(span_type=..., attributes=...)` | Auto-captures inputs/outputs/time |
| Code block span | `with mlflow.start_span(name=...) as span:` | Manual inputs/outputs required |
| Get active span | `mlflow.get_current_active_span()` | Use inside traced scope |
| Trace-level tags | `mlflow.update_current_trace(tags={...})` | Mutable, queryable |
| Async (function) | `async def` with `@mlflow.trace` | Context auto-propagated |
| Async (logging I/O) | `MLFLOW_ENABLE_ASYNC_TRACE_LOGGING=true` | Background, non-blocking |
| Trace sampling | `MLFLOW_TRACE_SAMPLING_RATIO` | 0.0–1.0, per-decorator override |

   - **OpenAI Agents SDK** (`openai-agents>=0.4.2`): `Runner.run()`, `Runner.run_streamed()`, async generators, SSE streaming

     - Added `self.mlflow = MLflowSettings()` to `AppConfig.__init__`.
     ```python
     class MLflowSettings(BaseSettings):
         mlflow_enabled: bool = Field(default=True)
         mlflow_tracking_uri: str = Field(default="https://api-dev.ixxdna.com/mlflow/")
         mlflow_experiment_name: str = Field(default="lexxi-ai-agents")
     ```

     - Core utility: `configure()` sets tracking URI, experiment, and injects `MLFLOW_TRACKING_USERNAME`/`MLFLOW_TRACKING_PASSWORD` into `os.environ`.
     - `agent_run()` context manager wraps agent calls, logs params on entry, auto-records `latency_ms` on exit, caller adds extra metrics to yielded dict. Graceful no-op when disabled or server unreachable.
     ```python
     def configure(*, enabled, tracking_uri, experiment_name, username="", password=""):
         global _enabled
         _enabled = enabled
         if not enabled:
             logger.debug("[mlflow] Tracking disabled")
             return
         import mlflow
         if username:
             os.environ["MLFLOW_TRACKING_USERNAME"] = username
         if password:
             os.environ["MLFLOW_TRACKING_PASSWORD"] = password
         mlflow.set_tracking_uri(tracking_uri)
         mlflow.set_experiment(experiment_name)
         logger.debug("[mlflow] Tracking configured", extra={...})

     @contextmanager
     def agent_run(run_name, params, tags=None):
         metrics = {}
         start = time.perf_counter()
         if not _enabled:
             yield metrics
             return
         import mlflow
         tracking_active = False
         try:
             mlflow.start_run(run_name=run_name, tags=tags or {})
             mlflow.log_params({k: str(v)[:250] for k, v in params.items()})
             tracking_active = True
         except Exception:
             logger.warning("[mlflow] Could not start run '%s'", run_name, exc_info=True)
         try:
             yield metrics
         finally:
             duration_ms = round((time.perf_counter() - start) * 1000, 2)
             metrics.setdefault("latency_ms", duration_ms)
             if tracking_active:
                 try:
                     mlflow.log_metrics(metrics)
                 except Exception:
                     logger.debug(...)
                 finally:
                     try:
                         mlflow.end_run()
                     except Exception:
                         logger.debug(...)
     ```

Second attempt: added `--allowed-origins` CLI flag — wrong flag name, server errored with `No such option: --allowed-origins`. Fixed by using the correct flag `--cors-allowed-origins`.
